bibliographic_data.title: Unveiling Palomar 2: The Most Obscure Globular Cluster in the Outer Halo
bibliographic_data.abstract: We present the first color-magnitude study for Palomar 2, a distant and heavily obscured globular cluster near the Galactic anticenter. Our (V, V −I) CMD, obtained with the UH8K camera at CFHT, reaches V (lim) ≃24 and clearly shows the principal sequences of the cluster, though with substantial overall foreground absorption and differential reddening. The CMD morphology shows a well-populated red horizontal branch with a sparser extension to the blue, similar to clusters such as NGC 1261, 1851, or 6229 with metallicities near [Fe/H] ≃−1.3. From an average of several indicators, we estimate the foreground reddening at E(B −V ) = 1.24 ± 0.07 and obtain a true distance modulus (m −M)0 = 17.1 ± 0.3, placing it ≃34 kpc from the Galactic center.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study provides the first color-magnitude diagram for Palomar 2, a distant and heavily obscured globular cluster in the outer halo of the Milky Way.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors used the UH8K camera at CFHT to obtain (V, V −I) CMD data reaching V (lim) ≃24. They calibrated their instrumental magnitudes against standard-star fields and performed photometry using DAOPHOT II and ALLSTAR codes.
outcomes: Determined a true distance modulus (m −M)0 = 17.1 ± 0.3, placing Palomar 2 ≃34 kpc from the Galactic center
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper presents the first color-magnitude study for Palomar 2, a distant and heavily obscured globular cluster near the Galactic anticenter. Using the UH8K camera at CFHT, the authors obtained (V, V −I) CMD data reaching V (lim) ≃24. The CMD shows a well-populated red horizontal branch with a sparser extension to the blue, similar to clusters such as NGC 1261, 1851, or 6229 with metallicities near [Fe/H] ≃−1.3. From an average of several indicators, they estimated the foreground reddening at E(B −V ) = 1.24 ± 0.07 and determined a true distance modulus (m −M)0 = 17.1 ± 0.3, placing Palomar 2 ≃34 kpc from the Galactic center.
